era_context: The Eastern Roman Empire under Justinian I has just completed the reconquest of North Africa from the Vandals (533) and is preparing for the invasion of Italy. The Northern Wei Dynasty in China is fragmenting into the Eastern Wei and Western Wei. The Sasanid Persian Empire under Khosrow I is consolidating power after internal strife. This is a transitional year between major military campaigns and political reorganisations across three continents.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia (limited sources beyond Chinese and Byzantine accounts)

events.title: Northern Wei Dynasty formally fragments; Eastern Wei and Western Wei established
events.description: The Northern Wei Dynasty, which had ruled northern China since 386, formally splits into two successor states. The Eastern Wei (534–550) is established in Ye (modern Henan) under the nominal rule of Emperor Xiaojing, with real power held by the Gao family. The Western Wei (535–557) is established in Chang'an (modern Xi'an) under Emperor Wen. This division reflects the fragmentation of the Xianbei-led state and marks the beginning of a period of intense competition between north and south China.
events.certainty_note: Chinese historical records clearly document the division of the Northern Wei. The year 534 is the conventional date for the formal establishment of the Eastern Wei.

events.title: Vandal War concludes; Byzantine reconquest of North Africa completed
events.description: The Byzantine general Belisarius completes the reconquest of North Africa from the Vandal Kingdom, which had ruled the region since 439. The final Vandal king, Gelimer, is defeated and captured. Byzantine forces restore Roman imperial control over the wealthy provinces of Africa Proconsularis, Byzacena, and Numidia. This victory is celebrated in Constantinople but proves difficult to maintain; the reconquest drains Byzantine resources and leaves the region vulnerable to later Arab conquest.

sources.name: Procopius, History of the Wars (Book III, De Bello Vandalico)

events.certainty_note: Procopius provides detailed contemporary accounts of the Vandal War. The conclusion in 534 is well-attested.

events.title: Khosrow I consolidates Sasanid authority; internal stability achieved
events.description: After suppressing internal revolts and establishing his authority over the Sasanid nobility, Khosrow I (r. 531–579) consolidates his power. He initiates administrative reforms, including the reorganisation of the tax system and the military. His reign marks a period of relative stability and cultural flourishing in the Sasanid Empire, though tensions with the Byzantine Empire remain high.

sources.name: Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings

events.certainty_note: Khosrow I's consolidation of power is well-documented in Byzantine sources. The specific date of 534 for the completion of internal consolidation is approximate.

events.title: Justinian I prepares for the invasion of Italy; military buildup in the Mediterranean
events.description: Following the successful reconquest of North Africa, Justinian I and his general Belisarius prepare for the invasion of Italy to reconquer the peninsula from the Ostrogothic Kingdom. Military forces are assembled, supplies are gathered, and diplomatic preparations are made. The invasion will begin in 535.

sources.name: Procopius, History of the Wars (Book V, De Bello Gothico)

events.certainty_note: Procopius documents the preparations for the Gothic War. The specific timing of preparations in 534 is inferred from the invasion date of 535.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of the Northern Wei fragmentation is sometimes overstated in Western historiography as a 'collapse'; in fact, the successor states (Eastern Wei, Western Wei, and later Northern Qi and Northern Zhou) maintained institutional continuity and were relatively stable. The Vandal War's conclusion is sometimes portrayed as a decisive Byzantine victory, but the reconquest proved unsustainable and the region was lost to Arab conquest within a century. The 'stability' achieved by Khosrow I should be understood as relative; tensions with the Byzantine Empire and internal factional disputes continued throughout his reign.
historiographic_note: The year 534 is well-documented for the Byzantine Empire and China, with contemporary or near-contemporary sources. The fragmentation of the Northern Wei is extensively documented in Chinese chronicles. However, sources remain heavily skewed toward literate, centralised states. The Vandal War is documented from the Byzantine perspective; Vandal sources are minimal. Sasanid sources are fragmentary. The experiences of rural populations, non-literate societies, and regions without surviving written records are largely invisible.

graph_edges.note: The Eastern Wei (established 534) was succeeded by the Northern Qi in 550.
The completion of the Vandal War in 534 enabled Justinian to launch the Gothic War in 535.
Khosrow I's consolidation of power in 534 enabled his expansion and military campaigns in the 540s.
